disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Linear regression models examining ACEs/bullying → internalizing/externalizing outcomes with coefficients reported (Table 2).
Multiple β coefficients with 95% CI reported in Table 2 for main effects and interaction models.
Original empirical analysis of ABCD data with regression models predicting mental health outcomes.
No evidence of duplicate; appears as original research article.

title: Prospective associations between media parenting practices and adolescent video game use.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Despite the rise of adolescent video gaming, evidence-based parenting guidelines and research on its specific behavioral impacts remain limited. This study evaluated whether media parenting practices are prospectively associated with video game use in adolescents 1 and 2Â years later. METHODS: We analyzed 7407 adolescents (51.6% male, age: 12.9âÂ±â0.6Â years) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (year 3: 2019-2021 to year 5: 2021-2023). Multiple mixed-effects ordinal logistic regression and generalized linear models assessed the associations between parent media practices (screen time modeling, mealtime screen use, bedroom screen use, use to control behavior, monitoring and limiting) and video game behaviors (mature-rated games, problematic use and weekend video game time) 1 and 2Â years later, adjusting for covariates. RESULTS: Higher parental screen time modeling, mealtime screen use and bedroom screen use were associated with higher odds of playing mature-rated video games, whereas higher parental monitoring of screen time and limiting screen time were associated with lower odds of playing mature-rated video games and less total video game use 1 and 2Â years later. Higher mealtime screen use, bedroom screen use and use of screens to control behavior were associated with greater total video game use 1 and 2Â years later. CONCLUSIONS: This study demonstrates that certain media parenting practices can reduce adolescent video game use, while low parental involvement is linked to more problematic video game use behaviors. This study shows that parenting practices, including screen modeling, may influence adolescents' video game behaviors.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Video game use (mature-rated, problematic, weekend time); qualifies per B6 (mobile/app gaming). Outcomes measured: frequency of mature-rated games, VGAQ (problematic use), total weekend game time.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Video game use only (gaming without social media or smartphone time). No qualifying SM variable (time on social media, platforms, etc.). No separate SM measure. B6 inapplicable: gaming alone without SM coefficient/separate measurement. Per B5, unspecified gaming (without SM) does not meet criteria.

title: Associations Between Adverse Childhood Experiences, Adolescent Screen Time and Physical Activity During the COVID-19 Pandemic.
abstract: OBJECTIVE: To determine the associations between Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs), adolescent screen time, and physical activity during the early COVID-19 pandemic. METHODS: Data (2016-2020) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study were analyzed. Linear regression analyses estimated associations between ACE score and screen time and physical activity in May 2020, adjusting for potential confounders. RESULTS: Of the 6749 adolescent respondents primarily aged 12-13, 81.6% reported a history of one ACE or more. In adjusted models, a higher ACE score was significantly associated with greater hours per day of screen time, with youth with â¥4 ACEs associated with 2.3 more hours of screen time per day compared to youth with 0 ACEs. In addition, the adjusted models found that a higher ACE score was associated with lower physical activity; youth with â¥4 ACEs averaged 0.8 fewer hours per week of physical activity and 0.5 fewer days per week of 60 minutes of physical activity compared to youth with 0 ACEs. Gender and race were also significantly associated with changes in screen time and physical activity. CONCLUSIONS: ACEs are associated with higher adolescent sedentary behaviors, particularly greater screen time, during the early COVID-19 pandemic (May 2020). Clinicians caring for youth exposed to trauma in the postpandemic environment should explore screen time and physical activity behaviors.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Screen time qualifying variable (self-reported hours across 7 modalities: gaming, texting, social media, video chat, internet browsing, streaming video). Applies B2 (per-item breakdown of screen time reported, not summed-only)
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Paper examines ACEs as IV predicting screen time as outcome. Screen time is measured (self-reported hours across 7 modalities: gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, internet browsing, watching TV/movies) but is the DEPENDENT variable, not an exposure. No qualifying SM/smartphone variable as predictor of a non-SM outcome. Applies B7 (screen time measured but not as exposure per inclusion criteria).

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Linear regression analyses with ACE score predictor and screen time outcome; multiple t-tests, correlation coefficients reported (Table 2, Figure 1)
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Does not qualify; IC2 not met. Paper uses linear regression examining ACE → screen time association, but screen time is outcome not exposure.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: β coefficients with 95% CI reported in Table 2 for ACE-screen time associations (e.g., unadjusted β=0.54 [0.36,0.71], adjusted β=0.44 [0.26,0.62]); p-values (<.001)
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Does not qualify; IC2 not met.
